dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 2 for abdominal pain and problems.
Hi, I'm Jordan with the rescue squad. What caused you to call 911 today?
Abdominal pain, it's been hurting for about 12 hours now.
And are there any other symptoms, like nausea or shortness of breath?
No nausea, no vomiting or diarrhea, and no chest pain or shortness of breath. I have been feeling a bit dizzy, and it gets worse when I stand or walk.
I understand you mentioned dizziness-have you had any recent falls because of this?
Yes, I've been falling more than usual recently. My last fall was on Tuesday.
Patty, can you tell me your name and the year?
I'm Patty, and the year is 2020.
And what's today's date?
February 7th, 2020.
Your airway is patent, and you're alert and responsive.
Your breathing is normal in rate and depth with no signs of cyanosis.
Your radial pulse is strong and regular.
Ma'am, we're going to take your blood pressure now. It's reading 169 over 94.
Your pulse is 80 and respirations are 16. Skin appears warm, dry, and normal in color.
Your oxygen saturation is at 100 percent.
Your body temperature is 98.7 degrees Fahrenheit.
We're checking your glucose now -- no changes noted.
On a scale of 1 to 10, how severe is the abdominal pain, and how would you describe it?
It's an 8 out of 10. It's sharp and located in my abdomen.
You also mentioned chronic neck and left shoulder pain-is that still present? How severe?
Yes, it's 10 out of 10 and also sharp.
Do you have any known allergies to medications or other substances?
No, I don't have any known drug allergies.
Can you tell me what medications you're currently taking?

Acetaminophen / butalbital / Caffeine / Codeine, trazodone (Desyrel), losartan (Cozaar), Amitriptyline / Perphenazine, pantoprazole (Protonix), Prednisone, Duloxetine (Cymbalta), quetiapine (Seroquel).
Do you have any past medical history to tell us about?
I have pulmonary hypertension, chronic pain, and hyperlipidemia.
Let me check your abdomen. It's soft, non-tender, and non-distended.
When did the abdominal pain begin, and what seems to make it worse?
It began last night around 2 A.M. I don't know what causes it to get worse.
How would you describe the sensation of the pain, and has it changed from previous episodes?
It's sharp and hasn't changed. I've never had it like this before.
Does anything like deep breathing or position change affect the severity?
It doesn't really get much worse, but I feel more dizzy if I move around too much.
Let me check your neurological status-no signs of stroke or focal deficits found.
Your Glasgow Coma Scale is 15-fully alert and oriented. You are aox4, by the way.
We are starting four-lead ECG monitoring to observe your heart function.
We've recorded a 12-lead ECG and noted an abnormal finding of abn Q in V1 and small R wave in V2 with lateral ischemic T.
There's also an undetermined junctional rhythm observed in your EKG.
Your latest readings are blood pressure 131 over 73, pulse 75, and oxygen saturation 99 percent.
We performed a stroke scale and it came back negative.
Any change in the severity or location of your pain since we started our assessment?
It's still an 8, and the location hasn't changed.
We're going to secure you to the stretcher using a 5-point harness for safety.
You'll be taken to [COMPANY_NAME] via non-emergent transport.
The EKG reveals a sinus rhythm at this time.
We'll use a 4-person lift to transfer you from the stretcher to the hospital bed upon arrival.
No changes in your mental status have been noticed during transport.
[COMPANY_NAME] was selected as the destination due to your symptoms and EKG findings.
A full report will be given to the hospital staff at [CITY].
Your EKG is being continuously monitored for any changes.
Anything new or worsening with your abdominal pain or other symptoms during the ride?
Still an 8 in the abdomen, same sharp pain. No other symptoms added since we started.
We followed the medical - abdominal pain (protocol 3 - 2) to ensure proper assessment.
You're well-secured now and we'll keep you stable during the ride.
Upon entry, you'll be moved to bed 19 using a 4-person lift from stretcher to hospital bed.
Earlier vital signs included a pulse of 80, respirations of 16, and blood pressure of 169/94.
Let me just confirm you're comfortable and know we're going to [CITY] now.
Your feet are resting on the stretcher and the harness is secure now-looks good.
We'll hand over documentation and confirm transfer with the hospital staff before leaving.
